topicArea: Crowds, the Revised Mob & the Argument of Assembled Bodies

questions.passage:
At six o'clock the platform holds two hundred separate people, each sealed in a private errand. A train cancellation later, the same bodies have become something else, a crowd, an entity with a mood, a density, and a direction of lean, and each person in it can feel the change without being able to say when it happened. Individuals do not add up to a crowd the way coins add up to a sum; somewhere in the accumulation a threshold is crossed and a new thing exists, and what to make of that new thing has divided observers for as long as cities have made it daily.

The classic tradition made up its mind early and grimly. The crowd, it held, is the individual dissolved: reason handed over at the edge of the mass, emotion spreading by contagion, anonymity licensing what the person alone would never do. In this picture the crowd is regression with a thousand legs, civilization's varnish stripped in an afternoon, and the vocabulary that survives from it, the mob, the herd, the beast with many heads, was coined almost entirely by people watching crowds from balconies, which is worth remembering when weighing its objectivity.

Sustained study has been unkind to the balcony view. Disasters, the supposed home of mass panic, turn out to be full of order: evacuees queue, carry strangers, return for the injured, and die, when they die, more often from courtesy than from trampling. Crowds are not normless but differently normed, a shared identity assembling fast among strangers, the pronoun shifting to we within minutes, and behavior follows the emergent membership. Panic exists but is rare, and the myth of the mad mob has a documented convenience: a crowd that is mindless by nature invites management by force, and authorities have reached for the diagnosis most readily when the crowd's demands were most coherent.

What crowds actually manufacture is intensity. The assembly generates an emotion no member carried in, the roar that lifts the stadium, the silence that deepens the vigil, feeling amplified by the visible feeling of others until it becomes a property of the room. People seek this, and the seeking is not weakness: to be lost in something larger is one of the durable human appetites, served across history by worship, concerts, and marches alike, and a psychology that can only describe it as dissolution has confessed the limits of its sympathy.

The gathered body is also a sentence in the civic grammar. Assembly speaks: a hundred thousand people occupying a square make an argument that no petition with the same signatures can make, numbers converted into visibility, commitment measured in shoe leather and risk. That is why crowd sizes are counted, disputed, and litigated with an intensity no other statistic attracts, and why dispersal is the reflexive first response of the nervous. The crowd is the megaphone of those issued no other, and everyone contesting the count knows it.

None of this launders everything a mass of people can do. Crowds harm, but the worst of it is misfiled: the crush is physics, not psychology, density passing thresholds where individual choice stops mattering, a failure of engineering and admission control rather than of character, and blaming madness has repeatedly buried the audit that would have saved the next crowd. Cruelty in groups is real, and its newest habitat is instructive: the online pile-on borrows the crowd's amplification while shedding its bodies, and with the bodies goes something regulatory, the shared risk, the mutual visibility, the face answerable to faces. The bodiless crowd keeps the roar and loses the reasons for restraint.

The platform empties; the entity dissolves back into errands. A culture's relation to its crowds is, at bottom, its relation to the public itself, and the fear of crowds has often been the fear of what assembled people ask. The task, then, is not to prevent the gathering but to build for it, in the literal sense of barriers, exits, and honest capacity, and in the civic sense of squares that expect to be filled. A city that cannot host a crowd safely has an engineering problem. One that will not host a crowd at all has a different problem, and it is not about engineering.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because collective effervescence serves one of the most durable human appetites, the pleasures of assembly decisively outweigh the physical risks that dense crowds occasionally pose.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The classic contagion theory of crowds was correct about online pile-ons even though it failed for embodied assemblies.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Crowd crushes result from density thresholds rather than mass madness, so crowd safety is best left to engineers rather than psychologists.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The mindless-mob picture has failed under study; crowds are differently normed, civically expressive assemblies, and the task is to build for them rather than prevent them.

explanations.A: This choice converts the fourth paragraph's sympathy into a risk-benefit verdict the passage never runs. Intensity-seeking is defended as a durable appetite, but the sixth paragraph files the crush as an engineering failure demanding audits, not a cost to be outweighed by pleasure.
explanations.B: This choice overdraws the online analysis into a vindication. The pile-on borrows amplification while shedding embodiment's restraints, a specific diagnosis about what bodies contribute; nothing rehabilitates contagion theory, whose balcony origins and managerial convenience the passage has already indicted.
explanations.C: This choice promotes one corrective filing into the thesis and adds a jurisdictional rule. The crush-as-physics point is real, but the passage's argument spans the revision of the mob myth, the crowd's civic speech, and the closing injunction, and it assigns crowd-hosting to cities and cultures, not to a profession.
explanations.D: Correct. The passage dismantles the balcony view with the disaster evidence, differently normed, the pronoun shifting to we, exposes the mad-mob myth's convenience to authority, credits the crowd's manufactured intensity and its civic grammar, files the real harms accurately, and ends on the double task: build for the gathering, literally and civically, since refusing to host crowds is not an engineering problem.

questions.stem: The primary function of the third paragraph is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: overturn the balcony view with the record: disasters full of order, crowds differently normed, the mad-mob myth convenient to authority.

choices.label: B
choices.text: concede that mass panic, while statistically rare, ultimately justifies the classic tradition's longstanding caution about dense assemblies.

choices.label: C
choices.text: explain the physics of crowd crushes that the sixth paragraph develops.

choices.label: D
choices.text: argue that evacuation protocols should assume cooperation rather than competition.

explanations.A: Correct. The paragraph opens by announcing the verdict, sustained study has been unkind to the balcony view, and delivers three blows: disasters full of order, queuing, carrying, courtesy; the reinterpretation, not normless but differently normed, we assembling within minutes; and the diagnosis of the myth's persistence, mindlessness invites management by force, reached for most readily when demands were most coherent.
explanations.B: This choice converts an honest concession into a rehabilitation. Panic exists but is rare acknowledges the phenomenon while removing its centrality; nothing in the paragraph restores the tradition's caution, whose vocabulary the second paragraph has already sourced to balconies.
explanations.C: This choice hands the paragraph the sixth's material. Density thresholds and admission control arrive three paragraphs later; the third's subject is the behavioral record and the myth's political convenience, not mechanics.
explanations.D: This choice extracts an operational recommendation from an empirical correction. The paragraph reports what evacuees do; it prescribes nothing about protocol design, which appears, and then only as barriers and exits, in the conclusion.

questions.stem: The description of the crowd as the megaphone of those issued no other, in the fifth paragraph, most nearly means that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: protest organizers rely on amplification equipment that authorities frequently confiscate.

choices.label: B
choices.text: crowds are louder than any individual voice, which is why stadiums generate roars.

choices.label: C
choices.text: for people lacking institutional channels of influence, assembly itself is the available instrument of public speech.

choices.label: D
choices.text: journalists and broadcasters cover dramatic street crowds far more readily than they cover carefully written petitions.

explanations.A: This choice literalizes the megaphone into hardware. The figure names assembly's expressive function, not equipment; confiscation appears nowhere, and the paragraph's machinery is counting and dispersal, responses to the argument bodies make.
explanations.B: This choice borrows the fourth paragraph's acoustics for the fifth's politics. The stadium roar illustrates manufactured intensity; the megaphone sentence concerns civic speech, the square occupied as argument, where loudness is visibility and commitment, not decibels.
explanations.C: Correct. The paragraph builds the civic grammar the phrase compresses: a hundred thousand people occupying a square make an argument that no petition with the same signatures can make, numbers converted into visibility, commitment measured in shoe leather and risk. Those with lobbies, columns, and offices have other instruments; the assembled body is the speech of those issued none, which is why the count is contested and dispersal reflexive.
explanations.D: This choice reduces the mechanism to media attention. Coverage may follow, but the paragraph's account of why crowds speak, visibility, measurable commitment, risk borne in person, operates on authorities directly, with journalists unmentioned.

questions.stem: According to the second paragraph, the classic tradition held that the crowd:

choices.label: A
choices.text: was dangerous primarily because its physical density could exceed the engineering thresholds of the spaces containing it.

choices.label: B
choices.text: generated powerful collective emotions that no individual member of the assembly carried into it alone.

choices.label: C
choices.text: dissolved the individual: reason surrendered, emotion spreading by contagion, anonymity licensing the otherwise unthinkable.

choices.label: D
choices.text: was best understood as a political argument made by those without other channels.

explanations.A: This choice retro-fits the sixth paragraph's physics onto the balcony theorists. Density and thresholds belong to the passage's own corrective filing; the classic tradition's indictment was psychological, regression and stripped varnish, not structural.
explanations.B: This choice hands the tradition the fourth paragraph's neutral observation. Manufactured intensity is the passage's account of what crowds do, offered with sympathy; the tradition's claim was harsher and different, the individual dissolved rather than the feeling amplified.
explanations.C: Correct. The paragraph states the doctrine in its own terms: the crowd is the individual dissolved, reason handed over at the edge of the mass, emotion spreading by contagion, anonymity licensing what the person alone would never do, regression with a thousand legs. The three mechanisms, surrendered reason, contagion, anonymity, are the tradition's complete machinery.
explanations.D: This choice attributes the fifth paragraph's civic grammar to the tradition that feared it. Assembly as speech is the passage's revision; the balcony view saw a beast with many heads, and the third paragraph notes its diagnosis arrived most readily when the crowd's demands were most coherent.

questions.stem: A city council denies a march permit, citing the danger of mob psychology once large numbers gather. Based on the third and fifth paragraphs, the author would most likely observe that the council:

choices.label: A
choices.text: has acted prudently, since the passage concedes that panic, while rare, does occur.

choices.label: B
choices.text: is deploying the discredited mad-mob diagnosis exactly where the passage flags it, against a crowd whose demands are coherent.

choices.label: C
choices.text: has understandably confused a psychology problem with the separate engineering problem of barriers, exits, and honest capacity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: should have consulted crowd-density models before ruling on the permit.

explanations.A: This choice stretches a rarity concession into a permit policy. Panic exists but is rare sits inside a paragraph demonstrating order in disasters; the passage's conclusion is to build for gatherings, not to license denial on the myth's residue.
explanations.B: Correct. The third paragraph supplies the diagnosis of the diagnosis: a crowd mindless by nature invites management by force, and authorities have reached for it most readily when the crowd's demands were most coherent. A march is the fifth paragraph's civic speech, numbers converted into visibility, so the denial silences the instrument of those issued no other, with dispersal-before-assembly as the nervous reflex perfected.
explanations.C: This choice offers the council a category error it did not make. The sixth paragraph's physics governs crushes at capacity, a real planning concern; the council's stated ground was psychology, the mob, and the passage's response to that ground is the myth's exposure, not a referral to engineering.
explanations.D: This choice substitutes a technical process for the civic issue. Density modeling belongs to hosting crowds safely, the conclusion's literal building; the council refused to host at all, which the final paragraph marks as a different problem, and not about engineering.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the third paragraph's claim that disaster crowds behave with order and cooperation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that evacuees interviewed after disasters consistently and substantially overstate their own helpfulness to strangers after the fact.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that rehearsed emergency drills consistently produce more orderly behavior than genuine unexpected emergencies do.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that some fatal crushes occurred at events with no emergency at all.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reviews of evacuation footage finding competitive shoving the norm from the first alarm, assistance rare, exit behavior unchanged by shared identity.

explanations.A: This choice impeaches one instrument, self-report, without supplying contrary behavior. Inflated interviews are compatible with genuinely orderly evacuations modestly embellished, and the claim rests on records of what crowds did, not only on what evacuees said.
explanations.B: This choice compares drills to emergencies, which any account expects to differ; the claim is that real emergencies show order, and drills being more orderly still leaves real evacuations as orderly as the paragraph reports.
explanations.C: This choice supports the passage's own filing system. Crushes without emergencies are the sixth paragraph's physics, density exceeding thresholds; their existence says nothing about how crowds behave when disaster strikes, the claim's actual subject.
explanations.D: Correct. The claim's content is behavioral, queuing, carrying strangers, courtesy, grounded in the record, and its mechanism is emergent membership, the pronoun shifting to we. Footage reviews finding shoving normative from the first alarm, assistance rare, and no behavioral trace of shared identity would contradict the record and disconnect the mechanism at once, restoring the picture the paragraph exists to overturn.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings would most strengthen the sixth paragraph's claim that embodiment supplies restraints the online pile-on lacks?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Studies finding confrontations harsher online than face-to-face between the same participants, cruelty falling when video restored visibility, anonymity constant.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that major online platforms profit substantially and knowingly from the sustained user engagement that public pile-ons reliably generate for their advertising businesses.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Surveys finding that most participants in online pile-ons later privately regret their own contributions to them.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that in-person protests have declined as online activism has grown.

explanations.A: Correct. The claim isolates embodiment, shared risk, mutual visibility, the face answerable to faces, as what regulates, and its standing confound is anonymity. Same participants harsher online, cruelty scaling with the removal of bodily cues, softening when video restores visibility, and anonymity held constant throughout: the design removes the rival explanation and manipulates exactly the variable the paragraph names.
explanations.B: This choice explains why platforms tolerate pile-ons, an economics of amplification, without touching what bodies contribute to restraint; profitable cruelty is compatible with any mechanism producing it.
explanations.C: This choice documents aftermath, not mechanism. Regret shows participants judge their own conduct badly in retrospect, which says nothing about whether present bodies would have prevented it; the remorseful can be as disinhibited as the shameless in the moment.
explanations.D: This choice tracks a substitution between forms of assembly, which bears on participation trends, not on the comparative cruelty of embodied and bodiless crowds; decline in marching is not evidence about what marching's bodies restrain.
